title: Keep right max
description: Delete nodes that have greater value on right

examples.explanation: Start with list: 12->15->10->11->5->6->2->3. Outer pointer at 12: inner scans 15,10,11,5,6,2,3; finds 15>12, so delete 12. List becomes: 15->10->11->5->6->2->3. Outer at 15: inner scans 10,11,5,6,2,3; all ≤15, keep 15. Outer at 10: inner scans 11,5,6,2,3; finds 11>10, delete 10. List: 15->11->5->6->2->3. Outer at 11: inner scans 5,6,2,3; all ≤11, keep 11. Outer at 5: inner scans 6,2,3; finds 6>5, delete 5. List: 15->11->6->2->3. Outer at 6: inner scans 2,3; all ≤6, keep 6. Outer at 2: inner scans 3; finds 3>2, delete 2. List: 15->11->6->3. Outer at 3: no right nodes, keep 3. Final: 15->11->6->3.

Start: 5->4->3->2->1. Outer at 5: inner scans 4,3,2,1; all ≤5, keep 5. Outer at 4: inner scans 3,2,1; all ≤4, keep 4. Outer at 3: inner scans 2,1; all ≤3, keep 3. Outer at 2: inner scans 1; ≤2, keep 2. Outer at 1: no right nodes, keep 1. No deletions, output same as input.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n²)
approaches.summary: For each node, traverse all nodes to its right to check if any has greater value. Delete current node if found. Simple but inefficient for large lists.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Delete Nodes with Greater Value on Right Using Brute Force O(n²)
ladders.desc: Complete solution to delete all nodes that have any node with greater value to their right in a singly linked list.
ladders.explanation: The brute force approach solves the problem by checking each node against all subsequent nodes to determine if deletion is needed. Intuitively, for each node, we need to verify if there exists any node to its right with a larger value. If such a node exists, the current node should be deleted because it doesn't satisfy the 'keep right max' condition. This is straightforward but inefficient because it involves nested loops: for each of n nodes, we potentially scan up to n nodes to the right, leading to O(n²) time complexity. The algorithm works by traversing the list with an outer pointer. For each node pointed to by the outer pointer, we use an inner pointer to traverse all nodes to its right. If any inner node has a value greater than the outer node's value, we delete the outer node. Deletion in a singly linked list requires maintaining a previous pointer to adjust links. After deletion, we must be careful to update pointers correctly without losing the rest of the list. The space complexity is O(1) as we only use a few pointers. This approach is foundational for understanding the problem but is impractical for large lists due to its quadratic time. It connects to more optimized approaches by highlighting the need to avoid redundant comparisons. Common mistakes include not handling the head deletion properly, incorrect pointer updates after deletion, and off-by-one errors in traversal. The intuition is that we're essentially filtering the list based on a condition that depends on future nodes, which naturally suggests a right-to-left or more efficient scanning method, but brute force implements it in the most direct way.

examples.trace: Start with list: 12->15->10->11->5->6->2->3. Outer pointer at 12: inner scans 15,10,11,5,6,2,3; finds 15>12, so delete 12. List becomes: 15->10->11->5->6->2->3. Outer at 15: inner scans 10,11,5,6,2,3; all ≤15, keep 15. Outer at 10: inner scans 11,5,6,2,3; finds 11>10, delete 10. List: 15->11->5->6->2->3. Outer at 11: inner scans 5,6,2,3; all ≤11, keep 11. Outer at 5: inner scans 6,2,3; finds 6>5, delete 5. List: 15->11->6->2->3. Outer at 6: inner scans 2,3; all ≤6, keep 6. Outer at 2: inner scans 3; finds 3>2, delete 2. List: 15->11->6->3. Outer at 3: no right nodes, keep 3. Final: 15->11->6->3.

Start: 5->4->3->2->1. Outer at 5: inner scans 4,3,2,1; all ≤5, keep 5. Outer at 4: inner scans 3,2,1; all ≤4, keep 4. Outer at 3: inner scans 2,1; all ≤3, keep 3. Outer at 2: inner scans 1; ≤2, keep 2. Outer at 1: no right nodes, keep 1. No deletions, output same as input.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Traverse and Delete Node if Greater Value Exists on Right
ladders.desc: For a given node, check all nodes to its right and delete it if any has greater value.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core operation in the brute force solution. Given a current node in a singly linked list, we need to determine if it should be deleted based on the condition that there exists any node to its right with a value greater than the current node's value. The intuition is straightforward: we scan forward from the current node, comparing each subsequent node's value. If we find a larger value, we delete the current node. This involves two main steps: scanning and deletion. Scanning is simple: start from the next node and move until the end, checking values. Deletion in a singly linked list requires adjusting the 'next' pointer of the previous node to skip the current node. Therefore, we must maintain a 'prev' pointer that tracks the node before the current one. If the current node is the head, deletion means updating the head pointer. The time complexity for this sub-routine is O(k) where k is the number of nodes to the right of the current node, which in worst case is O(n). Space is O(1). This feeds into the full problem by being called for each node in the list. Common mistakes include not updating 'prev' correctly after deletion, forgetting to handle head deletion, and not stopping the scan early once a greater value is found (though early stop is optional for correctness). The intuition reinforces that deletion depends on future nodes, which is why brute force is inefficient—we repeat scans. Real code pattern involves a while loop for scanning and pointer adjustments for deletion.

examples.trace: Given list 10->5->20, current node is 10. Scan right: start at node 5, value 5 ≤10, continue to node 20, value 20>10, found greater value. Delete current node 10. Since it's head, update head to 5. List becomes 5->20.
List 8->3->6, current node is 3. Scan right: start at node 6, value 6>3, found greater value. Delete current node 3. Prev node is 8, set prev.next to current.next (which is 6). List becomes 8->6.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Nested Loop to Check Each Node Against All Right Nodes
ladders.desc: Use an outer loop to iterate through each node and an inner loop to scan all subsequent nodes.
ladders.explanation: The central technique in the brute force approach is the nested loop structure. The outer loop selects each node one by one from the start to the end of the list. For each node selected by the outer loop, the inner loop traverses all nodes to its right to check if any has a greater value. This is the essence of the O(n²) time complexity: for n nodes, the inner loop runs on average n/2 times, leading to quadratic time. The intuition is that to determine if a node should be kept, we need to examine all future nodes, and brute force does this explicitly for every node. The algorithm: initialize a pointer 'current' at the head. While 'current' is not null, use another pointer 'runner' starting from 'current.next' to scan until the end. If 'runner' finds a value > 'current.value', delete 'current'. After deletion or if no greater value found, move 'current' to the next node. Deletion requires maintaining a 'prev' pointer for the outer loop to adjust links. The space complexity is O(1) as only pointers are used. This logic feeds into the key sub-routine by being the framework that repeatedly calls the check-and-delete operation. Common mistakes include not resetting the inner loop correctly for each outer node, incorrect handling of 'prev' after deletion, and off-by-one errors in the inner loop start. The intuition highlights the inefficiency: we're re-scanning the same right portions multiple times. Real code pattern involves while loops with careful pointer updates.

examples.trace: Outer loop: current=3, prev=null. Inner: runner starts at 2, values 2≤3, 1≤3, no greater. Keep 3, move current=2, prev=3. Inner: runner at 1, 1≤2, no greater. Keep 2, move current=1, prev=2. Inner: runner null, no greater. Keep 1. Output unchanged.

Outer: current=1, prev=null. Inner: runner at 3, 3>1, found greater. Delete 1 (head), update head=3. Now current was deleted, so set current=head=3, prev=null. Outer: current=3, prev=null. Inner: runner at 2, 2≤3, no greater. Keep 3, move current=2, prev=3. Inner: runner null, no greater. Keep 2. Final: 3->2.

ladders.title: Building Block — Deletion of a Node in a Singly Linked List Given Previous Pointer
ladders.desc: Delete a node from a singly linked list when you have a pointer to the previous node.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept is crucial for the brute force solution because after identifying a node to delete, we need to remove it from the list. In a singly linked list, each node only has a reference to the next node, so to delete a node, we must adjust the 'next' pointer of the previous node to skip over the node being deleted. The intuition is that we're essentially cutting out the node by linking the previous node directly to the next node. If the node to delete is the head (i.e., there is no previous node), we update the head pointer to point to the next node. The algorithm: given a 'prev' pointer (which may be null if deleting the head) and a 'current' pointer to the node to delete, if 'prev' is null, set head = current.next. Otherwise, set prev.next = current.next. Then, optionally free the memory of 'current' (in languages with manual memory management). The time complexity is O(1) as it involves a constant number of pointer assignments. Space is O(1). This feeds into the core logic by being the action taken when a greater value is found during the inner loop scan. Common mistakes include not handling the head case separately, losing reference to the rest of the list by not updating pointers correctly, and accessing 'current' after deletion. The intuition is that linked list deletion is a fundamental operation that requires careful pointer manipulation to maintain list integrity. Real code pattern involves simple if-else for head vs. non-head deletion.

examples.trace: Given list 10->20->30, prev node is 10, current node to delete is 20. Since prev is not null, set prev.next = current.next, which is 30. So 10.next becomes 30. List becomes 10->30. Node 20 is removed.
List 5->15->25, prev is null (indicating head deletion), current is 5. Set head = current.next, which is 15. Now head points to 15. List becomes 15->25. Node 5 is removed.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Traverse a Singly Linked List with a Pointer
ladders.desc: Move through each node of a singly linked list using a pointer.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation is the building block for scanning the list in the brute force approach. Traversal involves starting at the head node and repeatedly moving to the next node until reaching the end (i.e., a null pointer). The intuition is that a linked list is a sequential data structure where each node points to the next, so traversal is linear. The algorithm: initialize a pointer 'current' to the head. While 'current' is not null, you can access current.value or current.next, then set current = current.next to move to the next node. This allows you to visit each node exactly once. The time complexity is O(n) for n nodes, and space is O(1) as only one pointer is used. This feeds into the building block by being how we find the node to delete or scan right nodes. Common mistakes include infinite loops by not updating the pointer, accessing null pointers, or losing the head reference. The intuition is that traversal is essential for any linked list algorithm, and understanding it is key to manipulating the list. Real code pattern is a simple while loop.

examples.trace: Start: current = head = 5. Current not null, visit 5. Set current = current.next = 10. Current not null, visit 10. Set current = current.next = 15. Current not null, visit 15. Set current = current.next = null. Loop ends.
Current = head = 2, visit 2. Current = 4, visit 4. Current = 6, visit 6. Current = 8, visit 8. Current = null, stop.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Node Structure and Pointers in a Singly Linked List
ladders.desc: Understand what a node is and how pointers link nodes in a singly linked list.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept for the problem. A singly linked list is a data structure consisting of nodes, where each node contains two parts: a value (or data) and a pointer (or reference) to the next node in the sequence. The first node is called the head, and the last node points to null, indicating the end of the list. The intuition is that nodes are connected in a chain, and you can only traverse forward from the head. This is foundational because the brute force approach relies on traversing and manipulating these pointers. For example, in Python, a node might be defined as a class with attributes 'val' and 'next'. The 'next' pointer is what allows us to move from one node to another. Understanding this structure is key to performing operations like traversal, insertion, and deletion. The time to access a node is O(n) in worst case because you must follow pointers from the head. Space is O(n) for storing n nodes. This feeds into the basic operation by providing the structure we traverse. Common mistakes include confusing nodes with their values, not initializing pointers to null, or misunderstanding how pointers work. The intuition is that linked lists are dynamic and efficient for insertions/deletions at the beginning but inefficient for random access. Real code pattern involves defining a Node class and creating instances.

examples.input: Create a list: node1 with val=5, node2 with val=10, link node1.next = node2, node2.next = null
examples.trace: Define Node class with val and next. Create node1: val=5, next=null. Create node2: val=10, next=null. Set node1.next = node2. Now node1 points to node2, and node2 points to null. Head is node1. Traversal: start at node1, val=5, next points to node2; move to node2, val=10, next is null; end.

examples.input: Single node: node with val=3, next=null
examples.trace: Create node: val=3, next=null. Head is this node. Traversal: start at node, val=3, next is null; stop.

testCases.input: Node1 val=2, Node2 val=4, link Node1.next=Node2
Node1 val=7, Node2 val=8, Node3 val=9, link Node1.next=Node2, Node2.next=Node3
Node1 val=10, Node2 val=20, Node3 val=30, link Node1.next=Node2, Node2.next=Node3
Node1 val=6, Node2 val=12, link Node1.next=Node2
Node1 val=9, Node2 val=18, Node3 val=27, link Node1.next=Node2, Node2.next=Node3
Node1 val=3, Node2 val=6, Node3 val=9, Node4 val=12, link Node1.next=Node2, Node2.next=Node3, Node3.next=Node4

approaches.name: Reverse Traversal O(n)
approaches.summary: Reverse the linked list, traverse while keeping track of maximum value seen so far, and delete nodes with smaller values. Then reverse back to original order. Efficient single-pass approach.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Delete Nodes with Greater Value on Right Using Reverse Traversal
ladders.desc: Complete solution to delete all nodes that have a greater value somewhere to their right in a singly linked list.
ladders.explanation: The intuition behind this approach is that by reversing the linked list, we transform the problem from 'checking if any node to the right is greater' to 'checking if any node to the left (in reversed order) is greater'. This allows us to process nodes in a single pass while maintaining the maximum value seen so far. After reversal, we traverse from head to tail (which was originally tail to head), keeping track of the maximum value encountered. For each node, if its value is less than the current maximum, we delete it because in the original order, there exists a node to its right with greater value. If its value is greater than or equal to the maximum, we update the maximum and keep the node. Finally, we reverse the list again to restore the original order. This approach is O(n) time and O(1) space because we only use a few pointers and don't create any additional data structures. The connection to L1 is that this level integrates all sub-routines: list reversal, traversal with maximum tracking, and node deletion. Common mistakes include not handling edge cases like empty lists or single nodes, incorrectly updating pointers during deletion, or forgetting to reverse back to original order. The algorithm: 1. Reverse the linked list. 2. Initialize max_seen = -infinity. 3. Traverse the reversed list: a. If current node value < max_seen, delete node. b. Else, update max_seen = node value. 4. Reverse the list again. 5. Return the head. Real code pattern: def delete_nodes(head): if not head: return None; reversed_head = reverse_list(head); max_val = float('-inf'); prev = None; curr = reversed_head; while curr: if curr.val < max_val: if prev: prev.next = curr.next; else: reversed_head = curr.next; temp = curr; curr = curr.next; del temp; else: max_val = curr.val; prev = curr; curr = curr.next; result_head = reverse_list(reversed_head); return result_head.

examples.trace: Step 1: Original list: 12→15→10→11→5→6→2→3. Step 2: Reverse list: 3→2→6→5→11→10→15→12. Step 3: Traverse reversed list with max_seen: Start max_seen = -∞. Node 3: 3 >= -∞, keep, max_seen=3. Node 2: 2 < 3, delete (remove 2, list: 3→6→5→11→10→15→12). Node 6: 6 >= 3, keep, max_seen=6. Node 5: 5 < 6, delete (remove 5, list: 3→6→11→10→15→12). Node 11: 11 >= 6, keep, max_seen=11. Node 10: 10 < 11, delete (remove 10, list: 3→6→11→15→12). Node 15: 15 >= 11, keep, max_seen=15. Node 12: 12 < 15, delete (remove 12, list: 3→6→11→15). Step 4: Reverse back: 15→11→6→3. Output: 15→11→6→3.

Step 1: Original list: 5→4→3→2→1. Step 2: Reverse list: 1→2→3→4→5. Step 3: Traverse reversed list with max_seen: Start max_seen = -∞. Node 1: 1 >= -∞, keep, max_seen=1. Node 2: 2 >= 1, keep, max_seen=2. Node 3: 3 >= 2, keep, max_seen=3. Node 4: 4 >= 3, keep, max_seen=4. Node 5: 5 >= 4, keep, max_seen=5. No deletions. Step 4: Reverse back: 5→4→3→2→1. Output: 5→4→3→2→1.

ladders.title: Key Sub-Routine — Traverse Reversed List and Delete Nodes Based on Maximum
ladders.desc: Traverse a linked list while keeping track of maximum value and delete nodes with smaller values.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core of the full solution, where after reversing the list, we process nodes to identify which ones to delete. The intuition is that in the reversed list, nodes that were originally to the right are now to the left, so by tracking the maximum value seen so far from the start (which corresponds to the maximum from the right in the original list), we can decide to delete current nodes if their value is less than this maximum. This works because if a node has a smaller value than the maximum seen so far in the reversed traversal, it means in the original list, there exists a node to its right with a greater value. The algorithm: Initialize max_seen to negative infinity and prev pointer to None. Traverse the list with curr pointer. For each node: if curr.value < max_seen, delete the node by updating prev.next to curr.next (or head if prev is None) and move curr forward; else, update max_seen to curr.value, set prev to curr, and move curr forward. Time complexity is O(n) as we visit each node once, and space is O(1) using only a few variables. This feeds into L0 by being the critical step after list reversal. Common mistakes include not updating prev correctly after deletion, forgetting to handle the head deletion case, or incorrectly initializing max_seen. Real code pattern: def process_reversed(head): max_val = float('-inf'); prev = None; curr = head; while curr: if curr.val < max_val: if prev: prev.next = curr.next; else: head = curr.next; temp = curr; curr = curr.next; del temp; else: max_val = curr.val; prev = curr; curr = curr.next; return head.

examples.trace: Start max_seen = -∞, prev = None. Node 3: 3 >= -∞, keep, max_seen=3, prev=3, curr→2. Node 2: 2 < 3, delete: prev is 3, set 3.next = 2.next (6), delete node 2, curr→6, list: 3→6→5→11→10→15→12. Node 6: 6 >= 3, keep, max_seen=6, prev=6, curr→5. Node 5: 5 < 6, delete: prev is 6, set 6.next = 5.next (11), delete node 5, curr→11, list: 3→6→11→10→15→12. Node 11: 11 >= 6, keep, max_seen=11, prev=11, curr→10. Node 10: 10 < 11, delete: prev is 11, set 11.next = 10.next (15), delete node 10, curr→15, list: 3→6→11→15→12. Node 15: 15 >= 11, keep, max_seen=15, prev=15, curr→12. Node 12: 12 < 15, delete: prev is 15, set 15.next = 12.next (None), delete node 12, curr→None, list: 3→6→11→15. Output: 3→6→11→15.

Start max_seen = -∞, prev = None. Node 1: 1 >= -∞, keep, max_seen=1, prev=1, curr→2. Node 2: 2 >= 1, keep, max_seen=2, prev=2, curr→3. Node 3: 3 >= 2, keep, max_seen=3, prev=3, curr→4. Node 4: 4 >= 3, keep, max_seen=4, prev=4, curr→5. Node 5: 5 >= 4, keep, max_seen=5, prev=5, curr→None. No deletions. Output: 1→2→3→4→5.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Tracking Maximum While Traversing a List
ladders.desc: Maintain the maximum value encountered so far while iterating through a linked list.
ladders.explanation: This core logic involves traversing a linked list and at each step, comparing the current node's value with a running maximum. The intuition is that by keeping track of the largest value seen from the beginning of the traversal, we can make decisions about subsequent nodes, such as whether to delete them if they are smaller (as in the full problem). This technique is fundamental in many algorithms where relative ordering matters. The algorithm is straightforward: initialize max_seen to a very small value (e.g., negative infinity). For each node in the list from head to tail, if node.value > max_seen, update max_seen to node.value; otherwise, node.value <= max_seen, so max_seen remains unchanged. This runs in O(n) time and O(1) space. It connects to L1 by being the essential part of the traversal where we use max_seen to decide deletions. Common mistakes include initializing max_seen incorrectly (e.g., to 0 when list might have negative values) or not updating max_seen when values are equal. Real code pattern: def track_max(head): max_val = float('-inf'); curr = head; while curr: if curr.val > max_val: max_val = curr.val; curr = curr.next; return max_val.

examples.trace: Start max_seen = -∞. Node 3: 3 > -∞, max_seen=3. Node 2: 2 <= 3, max_seen=3. Node 6: 6 > 3, max_seen=6. Node 5: 5 <= 6, max_seen=6. Node 11: 11 > 6, max_seen=11. Node 10: 10 <= 11, max_seen=11. Node 15: 15 > 11, max_seen=15. Node 12: 12 <= 15, max_seen=15. Final max_seen=15.
Start max_seen = -∞. Node 1: 1 > -∞, max_seen=1. Node 2: 2 > 1, max_seen=2. Node 3: 3 > 2, max_seen=3. Node 2: 2 <= 3, max_seen=3. Node 1: 1 <= 3, max_seen=3. Final max_seen=3.

ladders.title: Building Block — Deleting a Node from a Singly Linked List
ladders.desc: Remove a specific node from a linked list given a pointer to it and the previous node.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves deleting a node from a singly linked list when you have access to the node to delete and its previous node. The intuition is that in a singly linked list, each node points to the next, so to remove a node, you need to bypass it by updating the previous node's next pointer to skip over the node to delete. If the node to delete is the head (no previous node), you update the head pointer to the next node. The algorithm: if prev is not None, set prev.next = node_to_delete.next; else, set head = node_to_delete.next. Then, optionally free the memory of the deleted node. This operation is O(1) time and O(1) space. It connects to L2 and L1 as deletion is used when a node's value is less than the maximum during traversal. Common mistakes include not handling the head case separately, losing reference to the list after deletion, or not updating pointers correctly. Real code pattern: def delete_node(head, prev, node_to_delete): if prev: prev.next = node_to_delete.next; else: head = node_to_delete.next; del node_to_delete; return head.

examples.trace: Given list: 1→2→3→4. Node to delete is 3, prev node is 2. Step: Set prev.next (2.next) = node_to_delete.next (3.next which is 4). So 2.next now points to 4. List becomes: 1→2→4. Node 3 is removed.

examples.input: List: 5 -> 10 -> 15, delete head node with value 5
examples.trace: Given list: 5→10→15. Node to delete is head 5, prev is None. Step: Set head = node_to_delete.next (5.next which is 10). So new head is 10. List becomes: 10→15. Node 5 is removed.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Traversing a Linked List
ladders.desc: Iterate through all nodes in a singly linked list from head to tail.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves visiting each node in a linked list sequentially. The intuition is that a linked list is a linear data structure where each node contains a value and a pointer to the next node, so traversal starts at the head and follows the next pointers until reaching None (indicating the end). This is fundamental for accessing or processing elements in the list. The algorithm: initialize a current pointer to head. While current is not None, process current node (e.g., print value, update variables), then set current = current.next. Time complexity is O(n) where n is the number of nodes, and space is O(1) as only a pointer is used. It connects to L3 as traversal is necessary to find nodes to delete. Common mistakes include infinite loops by not updating the current pointer, or accessing None values. Real code pattern: def traverse(head): curr = head; while curr: print(curr.val); curr = curr.next.

examples.trace: Start curr = head (1). While curr not None: Process node 1, set curr = 1.next (2). Process node 2, set curr = 2.next (3). Process node 3, set curr = 3.next (4). Process node 4, set curr = 4.next (None). Loop ends. Visited nodes: 1, 2, 3, 4.
Start curr = head (5). While curr not None: Process node 5, set curr = 5.next (None). Loop ends. Visited node: 5.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Linked List Node Structure
ladders.desc: Learn what a node is in a singly linked list and how nodes are connected.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept introduces the basic building block of a linked list: the node. A node is a simple data structure that contains two parts: a value (or data) and a pointer (or reference) to the next node in the sequence. In a singly linked list, each node points only to the next node, forming a chain. The first node is called the head, and the last node points to None (or null), indicating the end of the list. The intuition is that unlike arrays, linked lists do not store elements in contiguous memory; instead, they use pointers to link nodes, allowing dynamic size and efficient insertions/deletions. This is crucial for the full problem because we manipulate these pointers to reverse the list and delete nodes. Time and space are not applicable here as it's a conceptual understanding. It connects to L4 as traversal relies on following these pointers. Common mistakes include confusing nodes with their values or not understanding how pointers work. Real code pattern: class Node: def __init__(self, val): self.val = val; self.next = None.

examples.input: Create a linked list with nodes: value 1, value 2, value 3
examples.trace: Step 1: Create node1 with val=1, next=None. Step 2: Create node2 with val=2, next=None. Step 3: Create node3 with val=3, next=None. Step 4: Link nodes: set node1.next = node2, node2.next = node3. Now node1 points to node2, node2 points to node3, node3 points to None. Visual: 1→2→3→None.

examples.input: What is the head of list: 5 -> 10 -> 15?
examples.trace: In the list 5→10→15, the head is the first node, which has value 5. It points to the next node with value 10.

testCases.input: What does last node point to in 7 -> 8?
What is value of second node in 5 -> 10 -> 15?
What is next of head in 9 -> 8 -> 7?

approaches.name: Stack O(n)
approaches.summary: Use a stack to store nodes while traversing left to right, popping and deleting nodes when a greater value is found on the right. Maintains relative order efficiently.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Delete Nodes with Greater Value on Right Using Stack
ladders.desc: Complete solution to remove nodes that have any greater value to their right while maintaining relative order of remaining nodes.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires deleting nodes from a singly linked list when there exists any node to the right with a greater value. The stack-based approach provides an O(n) solution by processing nodes from left to right while maintaining a decreasing monotonic stack. The intuition is that we want to keep nodes that are 'local maxima' from the right perspective - essentially nodes that aren't 'dominated' by any node to their right. We traverse the list while maintaining a stack that stores nodes in decreasing order. For each new node, we pop from the stack while the top has a smaller value than the current node, effectively deleting those nodes since they have a greater value (the current node) to their right. After processing all nodes, the stack contains the final answer in reverse order, which we then reverse to restore the original order. The algorithm: 1. Initialize an empty stack. 2. Traverse the linked list from head to tail. 3. For each node: a. While stack is not empty and top of stack has value < current node value: pop from stack (this node has greater value on right). b. Push current node onto stack. 4. Reverse the stack to get the final list in correct order. Time complexity is O(n) as each node is pushed and popped at most once. Space complexity is O(n) for the stack. Connection to L1: This uses the stack traversal subroutine as its core mechanism. Common mistakes include not properly handling the reversal step, forgetting to update next pointers when building the final list, and incorrectly comparing values during the popping phase. The key insight is that we're essentially finding the 'next greater element' pattern but applied to node deletion.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Initialize empty stack []
Step 2: Process node 12: stack empty, push 12 → stack [12]
Step 3: Process node 15: top=12 < 15, pop 12 (delete), stack empty, push 15 → stack [15]
Step 4: Process node 10: top=15 > 10, push 10 → stack [15,10]
Step 5: Process node 11: top=10 < 11, pop 10 (delete), top=15 > 11, push 11 → stack [15,11]
Step 6: Process node 5: top=11 > 5, push 5 → stack [15,11,5]
Step 7: Process node 6: top=5 < 6, pop 5 (delete), top=11 > 6, push 6 → stack [15,11,6]
Step 8: Process node 2: top=6 > 2, push 2 → stack [15,11,6,2]
Step 9: Process node 3: top=2 < 3, pop 2 (delete), top=6 > 3, push 3 → stack [15,11,6,3]
Step 10: Reverse stack to get final order: 15→11→6→3

Step 1: Initialize empty stack []
Step 2: Process node 5: stack empty, push 5 → stack [5]
Step 3: Process node 4: top=5 > 4, push 4 → stack [5,4]
Step 4: Process node 3: top=4 > 3, push 3 → stack [5,4,3]
Step 5: Process node 2: top=3 > 2, push 2 → stack [5,4,3,2]
Step 6: Process node 1: top=2 > 1, push 1 → stack [5,4,3,2,1]
Step 7: Reverse stack to get final order: 5→4→3→2→1 (no deletions since no node has greater value on right)

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Stack Traversal with Node Comparison
ladders.desc: Traverse linked list while maintaining decreasing stack and popping smaller nodes.
ladders.explanation: This is the core subroutine of the full solution. The intuition is that we need to identify which nodes to delete by comparing each node with potential 'greater right nodes' as we encounter them. By using a stack that maintains nodes in decreasing order, we can efficiently determine when a node should be deleted. The algorithm processes nodes sequentially: for each new node, we check if it's greater than nodes we've seen before (stored in the stack). If the current node is greater than the top of the stack, that means the stack top has a greater value to its right (the current node), so we pop it (delete it). We continue popping until the stack is empty or the top is greater than the current node. Then we push the current node onto the stack. This ensures the stack always contains nodes in strictly decreasing order from bottom to top. The key insight is that we're building a 'rightward-looking' decreasing sequence - any node that gets popped has found its 'greater right node' in the current node being processed. Time complexity is O(n) as each node is pushed once and popped at most once. Space complexity is O(n) for the stack. Connection to L0: This subroutine produces the stack of nodes that survive deletion, which then needs to be reversed to form the final list. Connection to L2: This implements the decreasing monotonic stack pattern. Common mistakes include incorrect comparison direction (should pop when stack top < current, not >), not handling empty stack case, and forgetting to update node pointers when popping. The pseudocode: stack = []; curr = head; while curr: while stack and stack[-1].val < curr.val: stack.pop(); stack.append(curr); curr = curr.next.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Start with empty stack []
Step 2: Process 12: stack empty, push → stack [12]
Step 3: Process 15: top=12 < 15, pop 12 → stack [], push 15 → stack [15]
Step 4: Process 10: top=15 > 10, push 10 → stack [15,10]
Step 5: Process 11: top=10 < 11, pop 10 → stack [15], top=15 > 11, push 11 → stack [15,11]
Final stack: [15, 11]

Step 1: Start with empty stack []
Step 2: Process 5: stack empty, push → stack [5]
Step 3: Process 3: top=5 > 3, push → stack [5,3]
Step 4: Process 4: top=3 < 4, pop 3 → stack [5], top=5 > 4, push 4 → stack [5,4]
Step 5: Process 2: top=4 > 2, push → stack [5,4,2]
Final stack: [5, 4, 2]

ladders.title: Core Logic — Decreasing Monotonic Stack Pattern
ladders.desc: Maintain a stack where elements are in strictly decreasing order.
ladders.explanation: The decreasing monotonic stack is a fundamental pattern where we maintain a stack such that elements are always in decreasing order from bottom to top. When processing a new element, we pop from the stack while the top is smaller than the new element, then push the new element. This ensures the stack remains monotonically decreasing. The intuition is that we're keeping only elements that haven't been 'beaten' by a later, larger element. In the context of our problem, this corresponds to keeping nodes that don't have any greater value to their right. The pattern works because: 1) By popping smaller elements when we encounter a larger one, we're effectively marking those smaller elements as having a greater value to the right. 2) The stack naturally maintains the relative order of elements that survive. 3) The decreasing property means each element in the stack is greater than all elements that come after it in the original sequence (from its perspective looking right). Time complexity for building such a stack from n elements is O(n) since each element is pushed once and popped at most once. Space complexity is O(n) in worst case when the input is already decreasing. Connection to L1: This pattern is exactly what the stack traversal subroutine implements. Connection to L3: This builds upon basic stack operations. Common mistakes include not maintaining strict decreasing order (allowing equals), incorrect comparison logic, and not handling the empty stack case properly. The pseudocode: stack = []; for value in values: while stack and stack[-1] < value: stack.pop(); stack.append(value).

examples.trace:
Step 1: Start stack []
Step 2: Process 12: stack empty, push → [12]
Step 3: Process 15: top=12 < 15, pop 12 → [], push 15 → [15]
Step 4: Process 10: top=15 > 10, push → [15,10]
Step 5: Process 11: top=10 < 11, pop 10 → [15], top=15 > 11, push → [15,11]
Final: [15, 11]

Step 1: Start stack []
Step 2: Process 5: push → [5]
Step 3: Process 3: top=5 > 3, push → [5,3]
Step 4: Process 4: top=3 < 4, pop 3 → [5], top=5 > 4, push → [5,4]
Step 5: Process 2: top=4 > 2, push → [5,4,2]
Step 6: Process 6: top=2 < 6, pop 2 → [5,4], top=4 < 6, pop 4 → [5], top=5 < 6, pop 5 → [], push 6 → [6]
Final: [6]

ladders.title: Building Block — Next Greater Element with Stack
ladders.desc: Find next greater element for each element in an array using stack.
ladders.explanation: The 'Next Greater Element' problem is a classic stack application where for each element in an array, we find the first element to its right that is greater. This builds intuition for our main problem. The stack-based solution processes elements from left to right, maintaining a decreasing monotonic stack. When we encounter an element greater than the stack top, we've found the 'next greater element' for that stack top. The intuition: by keeping a decreasing stack, we're essentially delaying the assignment of next greater elements until we find suitable candidates. Algorithm: 1. Initialize empty stack and result array. 2. Traverse array from left to right. 3. For each element: a. While stack not empty and current element > element at stack top index: set result[stack_top] = current element, pop stack. b. Push current index onto stack. 4. After traversal, any remaining indices in stack have no greater element to right (set to -1). Time complexity O(n), space O(n). Connection to L2: This uses the same decreasing monotonic stack pattern but for a different purpose (finding next greater rather than deleting nodes). Connection to L4: This builds upon stack push/pop operations. Common mistakes include incorrect comparison direction, not handling indices properly, and forgetting to initialize result array. The pseudocode: result = [-1]*n; stack = []; for i in range(n): while stack and arr[i] > arr[stack[-1]]: result[stack.pop()] = arr[i]; stack.append(i).

examples.trace:
Step 1: Initialize result = [-1,-1,-1,-1], stack = []
Step 2: i=0, val=4: stack empty, push 0 → stack [0]
Step 3: i=1, val=5: top index=0, arr[0]=4 < 5, so result[0]=5, pop → stack [], push 1 → stack [1]
Step 4: i=2, val=2: top index=1, arr[1]=5 > 2, push 2 → stack [1,2]
Step 5: i=3, val=10: top index=2, arr[2]=2 < 10, result[2]=10, pop → stack [1]; top index=1, arr[1]=5 < 10, result[1]=10, pop → stack []; push 3 → stack [3]
Step 6: End: stack has [3], result[3] stays -1
Final result: [5, 10, 10, -1]

Step 1: Initialize result = [-1,-1,-1], stack = []
Step 2: i=0, val=3: push 0 → stack [0]
Step 3: i=1, val=2: top index=0, arr[0]=3 > 2, push 1 → stack [0,1]
Step 4: i=2, val=1: top index=1, arr[1]=2 > 1, push 2 → stack [0,1,2]
Step 5: End: stack has [0,1,2], all result values stay -1
Final result: [-1, -1, -1]

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Stack Push and Pop with Comparison
ladders.desc: Fundamental stack operations with value comparisons.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the basic mechanics of maintaining a stack while comparing values. A stack is a LIFO (Last-In-First-Out) data structure with push (add to top) and pop (remove from top) operations. The key operation here is: while the stack is not empty AND the top value is less than a new value, pop from the stack. This simple operation is the building block for monotonic stack patterns. Intuition: We're removing elements from the stack that are 'smaller than' the new element, which in our context means those elements have found a 'greater right element.' The algorithm: 1. Initialize empty stack. 2. For each new value: a. While stack not empty and stack.top < new_value: stack.pop(). b. stack.push(new_value). Time complexity depends on number of pops but amortized O(1) per element. Space complexity O(n). Connection to L3: This basic operation is repeated in the next greater element algorithm. Connection to L5: This assumes understanding of stacks and comparisons. Common mistakes include: incorrect comparison operator (< vs >), not checking stack empty before accessing top, and infinite loops if popping condition is wrong. The pseudocode: stack = []; for val in values: while stack and stack[-1] < val: stack.pop(); stack.append(val).

examples.trace:
Step 1: Start stack []
Step 2: Process 5: stack empty, push → [5]
Step 3: Process 3: top=5 > 3, so don't pop, push → [5,3]
Step 4: Process 4: top=3 < 4, pop 3 → [5], top=5 > 4, push → [5,4]
Final stack: [5,4]

Step 1: Start stack []
Step 2: Process 2: push → [2]
Step 3: Process 4: top=2 < 4, pop 2 → [], push 4 → [4]
Step 4: Process 1: top=4 > 1, push → [4,1]
Step 5: Process 3: top=1 < 3, pop 1 → [4], top=4 > 3, push → [4,3]
Final stack: [4,3]

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Stack Basics and Value Comparison
ladders.desc: Introduction to stack data structure and comparing numerical values.
ladders.explanation: This foundational level covers two basic concepts: 1) Stack data structure operations, and 2) Comparing numerical values. A stack is a simple data structure that follows Last-In-First-Out (LIFO) principle, with two primary operations: push (adds element to top) and pop (removes element from top). We can also peek at the top element without removing it. In Python, a list can be used as a stack with append() for push and pop() for pop. Value comparison involves using relational operators (<, >, <=, >=, ==, !=) to compare numbers. The key insight for our problem is understanding that when we compare two numbers a and b, if a < b, then b is greater than a. This simple comparison is the basis for deciding which nodes to delete. The intuition: if we're maintaining a stack and we see a new value that's greater than the top of the stack, that means the stack top has a 'greater value to its right' (the new value). Time complexity for push/pop is O(1). Space complexity depends on number of elements stored. Connection to L4: This provides the basic operations needed for the stack comparison pattern. Common beginner mistakes: confusing stack with queue (FIFO vs LIFO), not checking if stack is empty before popping, and incorrect comparison logic. The pseudocode: stack = []; stack.append(5)  # push; top = stack[-1]  # peek; stack.pop()  # pop; if stack and stack[-1] < 10: ...  # compare.

examples.input: Push 5, push 3, compare top < 4?, pop, push 4
examples.trace:
Step 1: Start empty stack []
Step 2: Push 5 → stack [5]
Step 3: Push 3 → stack [5,3]
Step 4: Check: top=3, compare 3 < 4? Yes (true)
Step 5: Pop → removes 3, stack [5]
Step 6: Push 4 → stack [5,4]
Final stack: [5,4]

examples.input: Push 2, push 6, compare top < 5?, pop, push 5
examples.trace:
Step 1: Start empty stack []
Step 2: Push 2 → stack [2]
Step 3: Push 6 → stack [2,6]
Step 4: Check: top=6, compare 6 < 5? No (false)
Step 5: Do not pop
Step 6: Push 5 → stack [2,6,5]
Final stack: [2,6,5]
